title: CarsAndBids Analyzer
description: Interactive analytics dashboard for 35K+ Cars & Bids auction listings. Cross-filterable views across market trends, individual listings, model comparisons, and a high-level overview.

detail.tagline: A self-updating sales-intelligence dashboard on 35K real Cars & Bids auctions.
detail.role: Solo — scraper, data pipeline, dashboard, and deploy.
overview: Cars & Bids publishes every auction result but gives you no way to analyze them in aggregate. This dashboard turns 35,514 real listings — 55 months of the enthusiast-car market — into something you can actually interrogate: which makes sell, where prices are heading, and how often a reserve price kills the deal.

Every chart is a filter. Click a make, a price band, a month, or a reserve status and the entire dashboard re-scopes to that slice, with selections persisting as you move across the Overview, Trends, Listings, Compare, and Insights tabs. It runs entirely in the browser off a prebuilt data file — no backend, no database.

gallery.caption: Overview — five headline KPIs over cross-filterable charts; clicking any element re-scopes the whole dashboard.
Trends — GMV, sell-through, volume and price over 60 months; click a month to scope every view to that window.
Compare — head-to-head make performance with a market-position bubble chart (sell-through × price, sized by volume).
Listings — all 26,497 sold auctions, searchable and sortable, with the priciest sales on top.
Insights — a data-journalism narrative on reserve-price failure and the revenue it leaves on the table.

features.title: Cross-filtering that persists
features.detail: Click any chart element — make, price band, month, reserve status — to filter every visual at once; the selection follows you across all five tabs.

features.title: Five analytical views
features.detail: Overview KPIs, 60-month Trends, a searchable Listings table, head-to-head Compare, and a narrative Insights story.

features.title: Self-updating weekly
features.detail: A scheduled scraper adds the latest settled auctions every Saturday and redeploys, so the dashboard is never stale.

features.title: No backend
features.detail: The whole app is static — a ~475 KB gzipped data file loaded straight into the browser — so it hosts free on GitHub Pages.

challenges.title: Sell-through rate that stays honest under filtering
challenges.problem: Sell-through rate is sold ÷ listed, but a price-band filter only exists for sold cars — so naively applying every filter would shrink the denominator and inflate the rate.
challenges.outcome: Filters are split into 'universe' filters (make, body, reserve, date) that change which listings exist, and 'sold-side' filters (price band) that only narrow sold cars. Rate-bearing charts use the universe set; value charts use the full set — so sell-through is always computed against the right denominator.

challenges.title: 35K rows, charting in the browser, still instant
challenges.problem: A 29 MB master CSV is far too heavy to ship to a browser, and re-aggregating tens of thousands of rows on every filter click risks a sluggish UI.
challenges.outcome: A Python build step packs each record into a 12-element array and emits a ~475 KB gzipped JSON; aggregations run over the compact in-memory set, keeping filter clicks instant even on a slow connection.

challenges.title: Keeping data current without a server
challenges.problem: A static site can't run a scraper on a schedule, yet the auction market moves every week.
challenges.outcome: A GitHub Actions cron runs a Playwright scraper each Saturday, dedupes new auctions by URL into the committed master CSV, rebuilds the packed JSON, and triggers a redeploy — a self-maintaining pipeline with zero hosting cost.

stack.name: React + Vite
stack.why: Component-driven UI with instant dev builds.

stack.name: Recharts
stack.why: Composable charts wired to the shared cross-filter state.

stack.name: Python + pandas
stack.why: Build-time scraping, dedup, and packing of the dataset.

stack.name: Playwright
stack.why: Headless scraper for weekly incremental auction pulls.

stack.name: GitHub Actions
stack.why: Scheduled scrape, build, and deploy — all free.
